medications.drug_name: salicylic acid topical keratolytic (over-the-counter wart preparation)
medications.indication_rationale: A topical salicylic acid keratolytic is the standard first-line, patient-obtainable, over-the-counter treatment for a plantar wart; applied daily to the lesion over several weeks it gradually destroys the infected hyperkeratotic tissue. Appropriate to advise in telehealth for a well adult as an application instruction, not a systemic dose.
medications.dose_route_frequency: salicylic acid topical wart preparation applied once daily to the centre of the lesion (after soaking and gently filing down the dead surface skin), continued over several weeks until the wart clears (per source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid on a diabetic / neuropathic / ischaemic (at-risk) foot or over broken/infected skin without in-person podiatry supervision

medications.indication_rationale: There is no bacterial infection to treat — no spreading erythema, heat, fluctuance, pus or fever. The pain is mechanical pressure on a wart. Treating a benign verruca as an infection is an anchoring error of commission; antibiotics are reserved for a genuine secondary infection if one develops.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply an over-the-counter aperture ('doughnut'-shaped) offloading felt pad around the lump so body weight is carried off it, for immediate pain relief when standing and walking, and wear well-cushioned footwear.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Offloading the pressure point instantly reduces the sharp mechanical pain and restores walking, without any procedure — the telehealth-appropriate substitute for the clinician's felt padding.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Soak the foot and gently file down the thick dead skin before each salicylic acid application, and persist daily for several weeks — plantar warts are stubborn and take weeks to months to clear.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reducing the overlying callus lets the keratolytic reach the wart and sets realistic expectations so treatment is not abandoned early.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not use a pumice stone or shared file/emery board on the wart and do not try to cut or dig it out; wear thongs/sandals in communal showers and changing rooms, keep the foot clean and dry, do not share towels or footwear, and watch the lesion for the infection warning signs.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Warts are contagious and abrading or sharing instruments spreads the virus to other sites and people; home cutting risks a laceration and secondary infection.

patient_education_points.point: This is very likely a common plantar wart — a harmless viral skin infection on the sole. On weight-bearing skin it gets pressed in and covered by hard skin, so stepping on it feels exactly like a stone in your shoe. The sudden pain is mechanical pressure, not a dangerous infection or something buried in your foot.

A doughnut-shaped offloading pad takes your weight off the lump and eases the sharp pain straight away; an over-the-counter salicylic acid wart treatment applied daily slowly clears the wart over several weeks.
Warts are contagious and stubborn: don't use a pumice stone or share files on it (that spreads it), don't try to cut it out yourself, wear thongs in public showers, and keep the foot dry.

See your GP or a podiatrist in about 2-4 weeks for professional paring or freezing (cryotherapy) if the over-the-counter treatment isn't working; seek care sooner if the foot becomes hot, red, swollen, oozing pus, throbbing at rest or feverish.

follow_up_plan.interval: routine GP or podiatry review in 2-4 weeks
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response to over-the-counter salicylic acid and offloading; confirm the diagnosis by paring/dermoscopy and consider cryotherapy or chemical cautery if the wart is not resolving; screen for any at-risk-foot factors (diabetes, neuropathy, vascular disease).
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Spreading redness, heat, swelling, pus or weeping, throbbing pain present at rest, or fever/chills; new numbness or known diabetes/poor circulation in the foot; or the lesion growing, bleeding spontaneously, ulcerating or changing in appearance.

must_include_items: Recognise a benign, likely viral plantar wart with mechanical pressure pain — not a retained foreign body or a deep-space infection
Advise a self-applied offloading (aperture / doughnut) pad for immediate pain relief
Advise an over-the-counter salicylic acid keratolytic applied over several weeks
Give contagion / self-care advice (no pumice or shared files, do not dig it out, thongs in communal showers, keep the foot dry)
Screen for and safety-net the at-risk (diabetic / neuropathic / ischaemic) foot
Provide a secondary-infection safety-net (spreading redness, heat, swelling, pus, rest/throbbing pain, fever)
Arrange routine GP / podiatry review in 2-4 weeks for definitive treatment (paring / cryotherapy) if OTC measures fail
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to a pharmacist for over-the-counter salicylic acid wart treatment and offloading pads
Arranging GP / podiatry cryotherapy or chemical cautery as definitive treatment
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net a secondary infection or the at-risk (diabetic / neuropathic) foot
Failing to address the immediate mechanical pain with offloading
Failing to give contagion / self-care advice, allowing spread to other sites or people
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging the benign plantar wart to urgent same-day in-person care, ED or 000 as if it were a foreign body or deep infection
Prescribing systemic antibiotics when there is no infection
Instructing the patient to cut, dig out or aggressively pare the lesion themselves with a blade
Claiming a paring / dermoscopy-confirmed diagnosis remotely when it cannot be done via telehealth
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising this as a benign, low-acuity dermatological presentation (a plantar wart with acute mechanical irritation) managed with self-applied offloading, an over-the-counter keratolytic, contagion/self-care advice, an at-risk-foot and secondary-infection safety-net, and routine GP/podiatry follow-up. The scored failures are (a) over-triaging the benign wart to urgent in-person care / ED, (b) prescribing antibiotics with no infection, and (c) advising unsafe home excision.
